architecture.summary: The paper studies parametric RAG (P-RAG), which encodes retrieved documents into lightweight trainable LoRA adapter parameters injected into a frozen LLM, rather than concatenating documents as tokens. P-RAG uses data augmentation (document rewrites + QA pairs) to train per-document LoRA modules via gradient-based optimization. DyP-RAG extends this by learning a hypernetwork that maps document embeddings directly to LoRA weights at inference time. The paper also evaluates hybrid PT-RAG, which combines parametric injection with explicit token-based context. Information flows through parameter updates to the FFN layers during generation, enabling knowledge integration without expanding context length.
trained_components: LoRA adapter modules (P-RAG: per-document; DyP-RAG: hypernetwork-generated)

biblio.title: Understanding Parametric Knowledge Injection in Retrieval-Augmented Generation

claims.statement: PT-RAG achieves the best overall performance across datasets when combining parametric injection with token-based context

claims.dataset: LLaMA3.2-1B average across four QA benchmarks
claims.statement: P-RAG does not consistently outperform T-RAG under HasAnswer accuracy metric, contrary to prior F1-based findings

claims.dataset: News25-QA (new dataset with 600 QA pairs, 807 passages)
claims.statement: P-RAG successfully encodes factual knowledge and significantly outperforms Vanilla LLM when tested on out-of-distribution news articles from 2025 (beyond LLM training cutoff)

DyP-RAG fails to demonstrate meaningful gains and shows no statistically significant difference from Vanilla LLM in new-knowledge settings, suggesting dynamically generated parameters do not genuinely capture document-specific factual content

Parametric representations exhibit specificity at a coarse level (relevant passages show higher similarity than irrelevant ones) but suffer from limited encoding fidelity compared to token-based embeddings, with heavily overlapping similarity distributions

Parametric injection increases knowledge contribution (measured by Parametric Knowledge Score) concentrated sharply in deep FFN layers, indicating it primarily provides high-level semantic guidance rather than fine-grained lexical details

Parametric RAG demonstrates improved faithfulness under knowledge conflicts (when retrieved evidence conflicts with LLM internal knowledge), stronger robustness to retrieval noise compared to T-RAG, and solid generalization to tasks beyond QA such as fact verification and slot filling

P-RAG alone is substantially weaker than T-RAG at consolidating evidence and reasoning over multi-hop passages; only when combined with explicit context in PT-RAG does parametric injection enable multi-hop reasoning gains
